statement: Icelandic public opinion polls consistently show a significant gap between support for resuming EU accession negotiations and support for actual EU membership. The Gallup Þjóðarpúls survey (February–March 2026) found approximately 52% supported continuing negotiations but only about 42% supported EU membership, with approximately 42% opposed and 16% undecided on membership. This ~10-percentage-point gap between 'negotiate' and 'join' has been a persistent feature of Icelandic EU polling since 2009. It reflects a substantial group of voters who favour exploring the terms of membership without committing in advance — essentially wanting to see the deal before deciding. The referendum question (whether to resume negotiations) therefore conflates two distinct decisions: authorising the process and endorsing the outcome.
source_name: Gallup Þjóðarpúls, February–March 2026; Capacent Gallup historical EU polling data (2009–2013)
caveats: Exact polling figures vary by pollster, date, and question wording. The 42/42/16 split on membership is approximate. The negotiations-membership gap could narrow or widen during the campaign. Historical precedent from Norway (1972, 1994) and Denmark (1972) suggests that EU referendum campaigns can shift opinion significantly. The undecided voters (16%) are likely decisive and may break differently depending on campaign dynamics.

related_entries.statement: The Gallup Þjóðarpúls survey (19 February – 4 March 2026, n≈810) found that a majority of Icelandic respondents supported holding a national referendum on resuming EU accession negotiations. According to the published results, roughly 60% of respondents supported the referendum, approximately 30% opposed it, and approximately 10% were undecided or did not respond. The survey found sharp party-based divisions: voters of Viðreisn (Reform Party) and Samfylkingin (Social Democrats) were the most strongly supportive, while voters of Miðflokkurinn (Centre Party) and Sjálfstæðisflokkurinn (Independence Party) were the most strongly opposed. The survey measured support for holding a referendum on resuming negotiations — not support for EU membership itself, which typically polls lower. This distinction is important: a voter may support giving people the chance to vote on the question while personally opposing EU membership.
